title: Epilepsy Surgery
subtitle: Block D — Functional Neurosurgery: Pre-surgical Evaluation, Resective and Disconnective Procedures, Neuromodulation

learning_objectives: Define the epileptogenic zone and its constituent zones, and explain how the pre-surgical evaluation attempts to localise them
Describe the components of the standard pre-surgical epilepsy evaluation (phase I non-invasive and phase II invasive monitoring)
Apply the Engel and ILAE outcome classification systems to rate post-surgical seizure outcomes
Explain the rationale, surgical technique, and expected outcomes for anterior temporal lobectomy (ATL) and selective amygdalohippocampectomy (SAH) for MTLE
Identify the indications for corpus callosotomy, hemispherectomy, and multiple subpial transection (MST)
Describe the mechanism and evidence base for vagus nerve stimulation (VNS) and responsive neurostimulation (RNS) as adjunctive neuromodulatory treatments

sections.title: The Epileptogenic Zone and Pre-surgical Evaluation Concept

blocks.content:
Epilepsy surgery aims to resect, disconnect, or neuromodulate the brain tissue responsible for seizure generation to achieve seizure freedom or significant seizure reduction. The theoretical framework underlying pre-surgical evaluation is the concept of the **epileptogenic zone (EZ)** — defined by Lüders as the minimum amount of cortex that must be resected to produce seizure freedom. Since the EZ cannot be directly measured (it is defined by its elimination), pre-surgical evaluation triangulates its location using multiple indirect markers: the **irritative zone** (cortex generating interictal epileptiform discharges, seen on EEG); the **ictal onset zone** (cortex where seizures begin, measured by ictal EEG recordings); the **symptomatogenic zone** (cortex whose activation produces the clinical seizure semiology); the **epileptogenic lesion** (structural abnormality identified on MRI — FCD, hippocampal sclerosis, tumour, etc.); the **functional deficit zone** (cortex showing hypometabolism on PET or hypoperfusion on ictal SPECT); and the **eloquent cortex** (cortex essential for language, motor, sensory, visual function — must be avoided).

The pre-surgical evaluation is conceptually divided into **Phase I (non-invasive)** and **Phase II (invasive)** investigations. Phase I includes: scalp video-EEG monitoring (ictal onset characterisation, seizure semiology analysis); high-resolution 3T MRI epilepsy protocol (1 mm isotropic MPRAGE, FLAIR, coronal hippocampal sequence for hippocampal sclerosis); FDG-PET (hypometabolism in ictal onset zone); neuropsychological assessment (lateralisation of verbal and non-verbal memory function, predicted post-operative cognitive risk); and functional MRI (fMRI) for language and memory lateralisation.

Phase II (invasive monitoring) is performed when Phase I data are insufficient or discordant — typically in the 30-40% of patients who are MRI-negative, when seizure onset is unclear, or when seizure onset zone is near eloquent cortex. Phase II options include: **stereoelectroencephalography (SEEG)** — multiple depth electrodes implanted stereotactically, targeting both cortical and deep structures (hippocampus, insula, orbitofrontal cortex, cingulate); **subdural grid/strip electrodes** — larger coverage of cortical surface, enabling cortical mapping via electrical stimulation; **intracranial EEG recording** from both options defines the ictal onset zone and guides resection boundaries.

Helps localise onset; not always the EZ itself

blocks.title: MRI-Positive vs MRI-Negative Epilepsy Surgery
blocks.content: The presence of a structural lesion on MRI (hippocampal sclerosis, FCD, cavernoma, low-grade tumour) is the single strongest predictor of favourable surgical outcome. MRI-positive patients with MTLE achieve seizure freedom in 60-80% (anterior temporal lobectomy). MRI-negative patients — where the cortex appears structurally normal — achieve seizure freedom in only 40-50%, and require Phase II invasive monitoring in virtually all cases. FDG-PET and high-density EEG source imaging add localising value in MRI-negative cases.

sections.title: Mesial Temporal Lobe Epilepsy and Surgery

blocks.content:
**Mesial temporal lobe epilepsy (MTLE)** is the most common surgically remediable epilepsy syndrome and accounts for approximately 60-70% of epilepsy surgery cases at dedicated centres. The classic cause is **hippocampal sclerosis (HS)** — neuronal loss and gliosis affecting the CA1 (Sommer sector, most vulnerable), CA3, CA4, and dentate gyrus regions of the hippocampus, with relative sparing of CA2. HS is identifiable on MRI as hippocampal atrophy (loss of normal hippocampal volume) with increased T2/FLAIR signal and loss of internal architecture on coronal sequences. The aetiology of HS is debated — an initial precipitating injury (prolonged febrile convulsion in childhood, status epilepticus, head trauma) may trigger a cytotoxic cascade leading to CA1 neuronal loss over years.

The clinical syndrome of MTLE typically includes: **aura** (epigastric rising sensation — most characteristic of mesial temporal origin; déjà vu; olfactory hallucinations); **complex partial seizure** (motionless staring, automatisms — oral: lip smacking, chewing, swallowing; manual: hand fumbling, repetitive motions; ipsilateral posturing of arm and dystonic posturing of contralateral arm); **post-ictal confusion** (typically 1-15 minutes — longer than in frontal lobe seizures); and **secondary generalisation** (tonic-clonic). Secondary generalisation typically propagates first to the ipsilateral temporal lobe then bilaterally via the corpus callosum.

The **Wada test (intracarotid sodium amobarbital test, IAT)** was the gold standard for pre-surgical language and memory lateralisation: sodium amobarbital injected into the internal carotid artery temporarily anaesthetises the ipsilateral hemisphere. Language lateralisation (left vs right hemisphere dominant): if injection into the dominant hemisphere causes aphasia, language is lateralised. Memory asymmetry: if the side planned for resection can 'pass' the memory test (the contralateral hemisphere can carry memory function), surgery is safe regarding memory. Wada testing is increasingly replaced by **fMRI language lateralisation** (IFG/Broca's area activation in a verbal fluency task) and **fMRI-based memory lateralisation** (hippocampal activation during encoding paradigms). However, Wada remains the gold standard at many centres for pre-MTLE surgery assessment of memory risk.

blocks.title: Clinical Context — MRI-Visible Hippocampal Sclerosis
blocks.content: The ideal surgical candidate for anterior temporal lobectomy: (1) seizures with mesial temporal semiology (epigastric aura, automatisms, post-ictal confusion); (2) EEG showing ipsilateral anterior temporal ictal onset (F7/F8 or AT1/AT2 electrodes); (3) MRI showing unilateral hippocampal atrophy with T2 hyperintensity (hippocampal sclerosis); (4) FDG-PET showing ipsilateral temporal hypometabolism; (5) neuropsychology showing ipsilateral verbal/non-verbal memory deficit. Concordance of all five modalities (electro-clinical-imaging concordance) = highest chance of seizure freedom (75-85% Engel I at 2 years with ATL).

sections.title: Surgical Procedures: ATL, SAH, and Outcomes

blocks.content:
**Anterior Temporal Lobectomy (ATL)** has been the standard surgical procedure for MTLE for over 60 years, refined by Falconer (1950s) and Engel (1970s-1980s). The procedure involves resection of 3-4 cm of the non-dominant anterior temporal neocortex (4-4.5 cm dominant) along with the amygdala, hippocampal head/body (2-3 cm), parahippocampal gyrus, and entorhinal cortex. The boundaries are anatomically defined by the temporal horn of the lateral ventricle (medial wall) and the boundaries of the middle temporal gyrus. The **Wieser modification** or **Spencer modification** defines the precise resection limits to minimise risk to: the Meyer's loop of the optic radiation (anterior to the temporal horn — risk of superior quadrantanopia 'pie in the sky' defect, occurring in up to 50-75% if the full temporal lobe is resected); the dominant hemisphere language cortex (particularly the superior temporal gyrus); and the posterior cerebral artery branches.

The landmark evidence for epilepsy surgery is the **RCT of surgery vs medical management** (Wiebe et al., NEJM 2001). This trial randomised 80 patients with MTLE to ATL versus continued medical therapy. At 1 year: **surgery = 58% seizure-free (Engel I) vs medical therapy = 8%** (P<0.001). Quality of life improved significantly in the surgical group. There was no significant difference in cognitive decline between groups. This remains the only randomised trial of epilepsy surgery and established the evidence base for surgical treatment of drug-resistant MTLE.

**Selective Amygdalohippocampectomy (SAH)**, developed by Wieser and Yaşargil, aims to remove the epileptogenic mesial structures (amygdala + hippocampus + parahippocampal gyrus) while preserving the temporal neocortex — potentially reducing neuropsychological morbidity (verbal memory, naming). SAH approaches include: **transsylvian** (through the Sylvian fissure), **subtemporal**, **transcortical** (through the middle temporal gyrus), and more recently **endoscopic** (increasingly used). Seizure outcomes of SAH are equivalent to ATL for MTLE at 2 years in most series (~60-75% Engel I), though some studies suggest slightly lower seizure freedom rates, potentially because neocortical components of the EZ may not be resected.

**Engel Outcome Classification**: Class I = seizure free (A=completely; B=non-disabling simple partial only; C=some seizures but seizure-free >2 years; D=seizure-free >1 year; sub-classes). Class II = worthwhile improvement (>90% reduction). Class III = worthwhile improvement (>50% reduction). Class IV = no worthwhile improvement. **ILAE 2001 Classification**: 1=seizure-free; 2-4=graded reduction.

blocks.title: Wiebe RCT (NEJM 2001) — Surgery vs Medical Management for MTLE
Visual Field Defect After Temporal Lobectomy
blocks.content: Meyer's loop (anterior radiation of the optic tract, swinging into the temporal lobe) lies anterior to the temporal horn and is at risk during ATL. Superior quadrantanopia ('pie in the sky' visual field defect — loss of superior contralateral visual field) occurs in 50-75% of patients after ATL if the full loop is resected. Modern neuronavigation and DTI tractography of the optic radiation can guide preservation. Patients must be counselled pre-operatively about this risk — a superior quadrantanopia can affect driving eligibility.

**Corpus Callosotomy** is a palliative disconnective procedure that sections the corpus callosum to prevent bilateral synchronisation of epileptiform discharges, primarily targeting **atonic (drop attack)** and **tonic-clonic** seizures. It does not cure epilepsy — the ictal onset zone remains intact — but prevents the rapid spread of discharges to the contralateral hemisphere via callosal connections, reducing drop attacks by 50-75% in Lennox-Gastaut syndrome (LGS) and other generalised epilepsy syndromes. **Anterior two-thirds callosotomy** (body and genu) is performed first; **total callosotomy** adds the splenium for refractory cases. Risks: the **disconnection syndrome** (acute: mutism, left arm apraxia, alien hand — transient, resolving within weeks); **left leg paresis** (supplementary motor area involvement during midline dissection). The complete disconnection syndrome after total callosotomy includes: left-hand ideomotor apraxia (cannot follow verbal commands with left hand), alexia without agraphia in some cases, and intermanual conflict.

**Corpus Callosotomy** is a palliative disconnective procedure for generalised epilepsy with drop attacks. **Hemispherectomy** (anatomical or functional) is indicated for catastrophic hemispheric epilepsy syndromes where one hemisphere is extensively damaged or dysplastic and the other is functionally normal: Rasmussen encephalitis (progressive unilateral encephalitis, anti-GluR3/anti-NMDAR antibodies), Sturge-Weber syndrome (unilateral leptomeningeal angiomatosis with cortical calcification), hemimegalencephaly, large perinatal ischaemic infarct, or extensive hemispheric FCD. Prerequisites: (1) seizures originating exclusively from one hemisphere; (2) contralateral hemisphere is functionally intact (particularly: motor, language — if dominant hemisphere resected, must be documented to have transferred to contralateral side); (3) hemiplegia or significant motor deficit already present. **Functional hemispherectomy** (anatomical preservation with complete disconnection) replaces anatomical hemispherectomy to reduce the risk of superficial cerebral haemosiderosis (the Rasmussen complication). Seizure freedom rates: 65-85% Engel I at 2 years. **Multiple Subpial Transection (MST)** is a technique for epileptogenic cortex within eloquent cortex that cannot be resected: horizontal cuts through cortical layers at 5 mm intervals disrupt horizontal epileptic propagation while preserving vertical corticocortical fibres essential for function (motor, language). Used for Landau-Kleffner syndrome (acquired epileptic aphasia) and focal epilepsy with seizure onset in primary motor or language cortex. Results are modest (~50-60% worthwhile reduction) but preserve function.

blocks.title: Disconnective and Palliative Epilepsy Surgery — Indications and Outcomes
rows: 50-75% reduction in drop attacks; not curative
Rasmussen encephalitis, Sturge-Weber, hemimegalencephaly, large perinatal infarct

blocks.title: Rasmussen Encephalitis — Surgical Timing
blocks.content: Rasmussen encephalitis is a progressive inflammatory hemispheric disorder causing intractable focal epilepsy, progressive hemiparesis, and cognitive decline. It is caused by T-cell mediated cytotoxic injury (autoantibody role debated). Functional hemispherectomy is the definitive treatment — surgery should not be unduly delayed because progressive neurological deterioration continues with medical management. The 'wait and see' approach risks greater functional deficit; earlier surgery (before complete hemiplegia) may paradoxically preserve better motor outcome by halting the ongoing inflammatory cascade.

sections.title: Vagus Nerve Stimulation and Responsive Neurostimulation

blocks.content:
**Vagus Nerve Stimulation (VNS)** is an adjunctive neuromodulatory treatment for drug-resistant epilepsy in patients who are not candidates for resective surgery or have failed it. The device consists of a subcutaneously implanted pulse generator (left infraclavicular) with a helical electrode wrapped around the left vagus nerve in the neck. Left vagus nerve is preferred because right-sided stimulation carries greater risk of cardiac bradyarrhythmia (vagal afferents to the sinoatrial node are predominantly right-sided). The stimulation frequency is typically 30 Hz, pulse width 500 μs, ON time 30 seconds/OFF time 5 minutes. The anti-seizure mechanism involves: vagal afferent stimulation → nucleus tractus solitarius → locus coeruleus (noradrenaline) → diffuse cortical desynchronisation. A patient-controlled magnet can trigger additional stimulation during an aura.

Efficacy of VNS from landmark trials and meta-analyses: approximately **50% of patients achieve >50% seizure reduction** (responder rate) with long-term VNS; approximately **5-10% achieve seizure freedom**. VNS is superior to medical management alone but substantially less effective than resective surgery. It is indicated for any drug-resistant epilepsy type where surgery is not appropriate, including Lennox-Gastaut syndrome, where it reduces drop attacks by 42-55% (EO3/E05 trials). Main side effects: voice alteration/hoarseness (most common — ~63%), cough, throat discomfort, dyspnoea during stimulation; depression improvement is a positive side effect (VNS is FDA-approved for treatment-resistant depression). The vagus nerve contains both afferent and efferent fibres — selective afferent stimulation is being developed.

**Responsive Neurostimulation (RNS, NeuroPace system)** is a closed-loop neuromodulation system that continuously records EEG from depth or cortical strip electrodes implanted at the seizure onset zone, detects epileptiform patterns using embedded algorithms, and delivers a brief stimulation burst (100-200 Hz, 1-2 mA, <100 ms) to interrupt the seizure before clinical manifestation. The RNS system was approved by the FDA in 2013 based on the pivotal trial (RNS Pivotal Trial, Morrell 2011) showing a 37.9% seizure reduction at 1 year vs 17.3% in sham group (P=0.012). Long-term data show progressive improvement: median seizure reduction 44% at 2 years, 60% at 6 years — unlike VNS (plateau after 1-2 years), RNS efficacy continues to improve, consistent with a neuroplasticity-mediated mechanism. Ideal candidates: bilateral MTLE (cannot safely resect both hippocampi), eloquent cortex onset zone, or previously failed resection.

blocks.title: Neuromodulation Options for Drug-Resistant Epilepsy
rows: Open-loop vagal afferent stimulation → cortical desynchronisation
Any drug-resistant epilepsy; Lennox-Gastaut; not surgical candidate
Closed-loop: detect ictal → stimulate onset zone
Bilateral MTLE; eloquent cortex onset; failed resection
Focal epilepsy, not candidate for resection; MTLE
Laser-induced thermal ablation of epileptogenic zone (MTLE)

blocks.title: Clinical Context — RNS for Bilateral MTLE
blocks.content: A 35-year-old with bilateral hippocampal sclerosis and bilateral ictal onset on scalp EEG cannot undergo unilateral ATL safely (resecting both hippocampi causes Korsakoff-type amnesia). RNS with bilateral hippocampal depth electrodes provides a non-destructive, reversible neuromodulation option. The device records from and stimulates both mesial temporal regions simultaneously. At 6 years, ~60% of bilateral MTLE patients achieve >50% seizure reduction with RNS — making it the preferred neuromodulation option for bilateral MTLE after demonstration of bilateral onset.

sections.title: Focal Cortical Dysplasia and MRI-Negative Epilepsy

blocks.content:
**Focal Cortical Dysplasia (FCD)** is the most common cause of drug-resistant focal epilepsy and the most common surgically treated epilepsy in children. The ILAE Classification (Blümcke 2011) defines three types: **FCD Type I** (isolated, architectural dysplasia — abnormal cortical layering, radial or tangential): mildest form, often temporal, may be MRI-invisible; **FCD Type II** (isolated, with dysmorphic neurons and/or balloon cells): most epileptogenic, most common surgical subtype, typically extratemporal (frontal), characterised by the 'transmantle sign' on FLAIR MRI (abnormal signal extending radially from the cortex to the ventricle); **FCD Type III** (associated with a principal lesion — hippocampal sclerosis + FCD = Type IIIa; FCD adjacent to tumour = IIIb; FCD adjacent to vascular malformation = IIIc; FCD adjacent to other lesion = IIId).

FCD is frequently MRI-invisible, particularly FCD Type I and some Type II lesions — accounting for the majority of MRI-negative focal epilepsy cases. Advanced MRI post-processing techniques improve detection: morphometric analysis programs (MAP, MRISTAT), automated detection pipelines using machine learning (MELD project — multi-site AI-based FCD detection), surface rendering and cortical thickness analysis. **FDG-PET** typically shows focal hypometabolism overlying FCD. **18F-fluoroethyl-tyrosine (FET-PET)** and **11C-flumazenil PET** can identify FCD through GABA receptor density changes. High-density EEG source imaging and ictal SPECT with SISCOM (subtraction ictal SPECT co-registered to MRI) add further localising value.

Surgical outcomes for FCD depend heavily on lesion type, visibility, and completeness of resection. **Complete resection of visible FCD Type II**: 60-80% Engel I at 2 years. **MRI-negative FCD**: 40-50% Engel I — significantly lower. The key predictor of surgical success is complete resection guided by concordant EEG (ictal onset zone fully resected). Incomplete resection due to proximity to eloquent cortex is the most common cause of surgical failure in FCD surgery.

blocks.title: FCD Type II — Transmantle Sign
blocks.content: FCD Type IIb (with balloon cells) is characterised on MRI by the 'transmantle sign': a funnel-shaped T2/FLAIR hyperintensity extending radially from the cortex through the white matter to the lateral ventricle wall. This represents the arrested migration pathway of dysplastic cells from the germinal matrix. The transmantle sign is pathognomonic of FCD Type II and, when identified, strongly supports surgical candidacy with expectation of good outcome (60-80% Engel I) after complete resection.

sections.title: Complications, Cognitive Outcomes, and Candidacy Criteria

blocks.content:
Epilepsy surgery carries a risk of neurological deficit that must be weighed against the potential for seizure freedom. The major risks of **anterior temporal lobectomy** include: superior quadrantanopia (50-75% with full temporal lobe resection — visual field testing mandatory pre- and post-operatively); verbal memory decline (particularly after dominant hemisphere ATL — 30-40% of patients have measurable verbal memory decline at 1 year, though often within normal limits); naming difficulties (anomia — more pronounced after dominant temporal resection); and post-operative mood disorder (depression in 15-25% in the early post-operative period, improved with successful seizure control at 1 year).

**Hemispherectomy** risks in children: hydrocephalus requiring VP shunt (25-30%); superficial cerebral haemosiderosis (haemosiderin deposition from repeated micro-bleeds — the complication that drove development of functional hemispherectomy — now substantially reduced); motor decline (worsening of pre-existing hemiparesis — expected, but should not produce NEW complete hemiplegia if the motor cortex was already dysfunctional). Hemispheric transfer of language function occurs in young children before hemispherectomy, enabling language recovery after dominant hemisphere removal — this does NOT occur in adults or older children.

The concept of **surgical candidacy** involves a multidisciplinary epilepsy surgery team decision considering: duration of drug-resistant epilepsy (two adequate drug trials at therapeutic doses = drug-resistant per ILAE 2010 definition); expected benefit from surgery (localised EZ, MRI positive, concordant data); expected risk of surgery (proximity to eloquent cortex, bilateral temporal disease, medical comorbidities); patient and family expectations; and quality of life impact of continued seizures. The **opportunity cost of delayed referral** is increasingly recognised — each year of drug-resistant seizures increases risk of SUDEP (Sudden Unexpected Death in Epilepsy), injury, cognitive decline, and psychosocial impairment. Early referral to an epilepsy surgery centre after two drug failures is recommended by NICE NG217 (2022).
